title: 16. Comparative genomics cross-species
desc: If the N-terminus is dispensable, we'd expect: (a) lower conservation than the C-terminal, (b) no recognized domain, and (c) species that hear without STRC at all. We checked ~180 STRC orthologs across vertebrates.
finding1: Among all Aves, only the North Island brown kiwi has a STRC gene (1,777 aa). Chickens, songbirds, and owls have no STRC gene at all — yet have excellent hearing (125 Hz - 8 kHz+). Stereocilin is not universally required for vertebrate hearing. It is specific to the mammalian OHC horizontal top connector / tectorial membrane attachment system.
finding2_title: Pfam domain boundary = our cut point
finding2: The Stereocilin LRR domain (PF21058) maps to human residues ~700-1100 across all annotated orthologs. The N-terminal ~700 residues contain no recognized Pfam domain. Our truncation at residue 700 falls exactly at the domain boundary — we remove the domain-free region and keep everything with structural annotation.
finding3_title: Fish STRC is longer, not shorter
finding3: Bony fish stereocilins range from 1,900-2,700 aa — up to 900 aa longer than human. The extra length is in the N-terminal region. This demonstrates the N-terminus tolerates massive size variation (±900 aa) across vertebrate evolution without losing function.
finding4_title: Dual-AAV papers converge on the same boundary
finding4: Bhatt et al. (2022, Science Advances) independently chose their dual-AAV split at Ser746/Cys747 to avoid disrupting structural domains. Iranfar et al. (2026) used a similar split. Two independent groups, designing for different purposes, identified the same domain boundary that AlphaFold 3's disorder profile predicts.
conclusion: No vertebrate has a naturally truncated STRC missing only residues 1-700 while retaining 700-1775. But the convergent evidence — no N-terminal domain, precise Pfam boundary match, bird STRC absence, fish length variation, and independent dual-AAV split point selection — supports the hypothesis that the N-terminal region is not essential for the C-terminal core's structural function.
